text:
Boxer Imane Khelif has won gold at the Paris Olympics in a defeat over China’s Liu Yang, ensuring the scandal surrounding her eligibility to compete in women’s events will continue.

Controversial boxer Imane Khelif has won gold at the Paris Olympics, ensuring the furore surrounding her eligibility to compete in women’s events will continue long after the Games are finished.

Khelif defeated China’s Liu Yang in front of a packed house at Roland Garros.

Most of about 15,000 fans who filled Court Philippe Chatrier at the famous French tennis venue were there to support the Algerian boxer, who has been at the centre of one of sport’s biggest controversies.

There was a deafening roar when Khelif was introduced to the ring, and boos for her Chinese opponent.

They chanted “Imane, Imane, Imane” throughout the bout and roared in approval when the Algerian delivered a flurry of blows to her opponent’s head in the first round.

Khelif never looked troubled in the gold medal bout and was declared the unanimous victor.

But her gold medal is sure to provoke further debate about whether she should have been allowed to compete in Paris.

Khelif and Taiwan’s Lin Yu Ting were disqualified from last year’s world championships after the International Boxing Association said they had failed gender eligibility tests.

The International Olympic Committee (IOC) allowed them to compete at the Paris Games, saying the boxers were born and raised as women, had been boxing in women’s events for more than six years, and satisfied all requirements to compete at the Olympics.

The IOC said in a statement last week said an athlete’s gender was determined by their passport and the boxers competing in Paris had passports that indicated they were female.

The Olympic committee has been locked in a bitter feud over the issue with the International Boxing Association (IBA), which it permanently expelled last year due to governance issues.

At an extraordinary press conference in Paris this week, IBA president Umar Kremlev, who has close ties to Russian president Vladimir Putin, accused the IOC of corruption and said it was destroying women’s sport by allowing the boxers to compete in Paris.

The issue has divided the boxing community and drawn furious responses both in support of the boxers, and from those who believe it is unsafe for them to be fighting at the Olympics.

Italian fighter Angela Carini withdrew from her round-of 16 clash with Khelif after just 46 seconds, saying she had never been hit so hard and feared for her safety.

Khelif’s Algerian team-mate told Sky News this week her compatriot was the victim of the war playing out between the IBA and the IOC.

“Imane is a woman and she’s not trans and we don’t do this in our country,” she said.

"They are lying.  There is problem between IBA and IOC and Imane is paying for this problem.

"It's insane and they don't need to do that."

But Sharron Davies, a former British Olympic medallist, said the IOC had shown “genuine neglect” towards the other women fighting at the Games.

"In boxing, it is insane to put a male in a ring with a female and for the IOC to say the only thing that's important to them is what is on a passport is genuine neglect,” she said.

”The duty of care has been thrown out the window, and my head wants to explode... We're literally on the cusp of a female athlete potentially being killed if we're not careful. It really is that bad."
